summary: When I was about two and a half, my family moved to the United Arab Emirates because my parents were missionaries and church leaders there. We first lived in Al Ain, and later I spent my fourth through eighth grades in Abu Dhabi, which really felt like my hometown. I also returned to the UAE during college to work on a hospital project in Al Ain. The UAE was a big part of my childhood and early adulthood.

Al Ain was the first city we lived in when we moved to the United Arab Emirates in the mid-1960s. My earliest memories are from the mission hospital compound there, with camels wandering around and red sand everywhere. Later, during college, I returned to Al Ain to work on building a new hospital. Al Ain holds a special place in my memories of growing up abroad.

In 1967, we moved to Jordan for one year so my parents could study Arabic at a special language school. It was a brief but important time because it helped my parents improve their language skills for their missionary work. After that year, we returned to the UAE. Jordan was a key stop in our journey living in the Middle East.

I traveled with my mom to Pakistan to take my brothers to boarding school, and I ended up staying there for a few years myself. I returned there again after ninth grade and stayed until I graduated high school in 1981. Pakistan was a major part of my education and growing up overseas.

I lived in Abu Dhabi from fourth grade through eighth grade and went to the American School of Abu Dhabi. I have my strongest childhood memories from there, like riding my bike around and feeling like it was my hometown. It was also where I developed my love for photography and traveling.

I attended the American School of Abu Dhabi during my time living in the capital of the UAE, from fourth through eighth grade. It was a formative place for me, where I felt at home and started to develop interests like photography. This school was central to my childhood experience in Abu Dhabi.

I was born in Northern Wisconsin in June 1963 and lived there for the first two years of my life before moving abroad. Later, I returned to Wisconsin for ninth grade and then again for college at the University of Wisconsin–Madison. I've stayed in Madison for many years now, raising my family and watching my daughters grow up.

I started college at LeTourneau University in Texas studying civil engineering but quickly realized it wasn't the right fit. I transferred to the University of Wisconsin–Madison during my first year. LeTourneau was my first college experience, but I found my academic path changed soon after.

I transferred to the University of Wisconsin–Madison after starting at LeTourneau University. While there, I switched my major to South Asian Studies after working on a hospital project in Al Ain. I also spent my last year studying abroad in Varanasi, India, focusing on Hindi and Urdu languages. Madison has been my home for many years since then.

During my last year at the University of Wisconsin–Madison, I studied abroad in Varanasi, India. It was an incredible experience where I focused on learning the written Hindi language as well as Urdu. This time deepened my connection to South Asian culture and languages.

India became an important part of my education when I studied abroad in Varanasi during my last year of college. I immersed myself in learning Hindi and Urdu languages there. My experiences in India helped shape my academic focus on South Asian studies.

While studying abroad in Varanasi, India, I studied the written Hindi language extensively. Learning Hindi was part of my broader focus on South Asian languages and culture during my college years. It was a challenging but rewarding experience.

Alongside Hindi, I also studied the Urdu language during my time in Varanasi, India. Urdu was part of my academic interest in South Asian languages while at the University of Wisconsin–Madison. This study helped me better understand the cultural and linguistic diversity of the region.

While working for a company called 2100, I attended a conference in Thailand focused on cross-cultural communication. It was there that I met Carol, a journalist and missionary's kid from Malaysia. That meeting in Thailand was the beginning of our relationship.

I met Carol in Thailand at a conference about cross-cultural communication. She was a journalist and missionary's kid from Malaysia with an interest in photography, just like me. We spent a lot of time together, kept in touch by letters and calls, and eventually got married in Kuala Lumpur. Carol had never been to the States before, and we decided to live in the US initially to start our family.

Carol, my wife, is from Malaysia, and we got married in her hometown of Kuala Lumpur. Although she had never been to the United States before, we decided to live there for a few years to start our family. Malaysia remains an important part of our story and heritage.

Kuala Lumpur is where Carol's family is from and where we got married. After meeting in Thailand and reconnecting in the Philippines, we planned our wedding in Kuala Lumpur. It was meaningful to marry in her hometown surrounded by her family.

I met up with Carol again in the Philippines at another conference while working with 2100. It was there that I proposed to her, and she said yes. We spent a few more months in the region planning our wedding before heading to Malaysia for the ceremony.
